metadata.name: Deeva Cintron
metadata.protocol: 4-direction expert panel verification (Demographer, Behavioral Economist, Political Scientist, Psychologist)

direction_1_seed_vs_interview.description: Seed data compared against interview transcript for consistency, enrichment, and contradiction.

demographer.detail: Seed states age 85, Female, married_present, Saline MI 48176, some_college, not_in_workforce. Interview confirms all: DOB March 14 1941 (Q85), references being 85 throughout (Q01, Q27, Q45, Q109), married to Harold 64 years (Q84), lives in Saline (Q08), attended community college but did not finish (Q90), no paid work (Q69-Q72).

Seed states 'mid-western white family in Michigan' with 'second-generation descendants of German immigrants.' Interview enriches: born in Tecumseh MI (Q87), father worked at grain elevator/feed mill (Q106), mother worked at Woolworth's five-and-dime (Q103), both parents had high school education (Q100, Q101), family emphasized thrift, hard work, church attendance, and Sunday dinners (Q01).

Seed mentions marital status 'married_present.' Interview provides extensive context: married Harold in 1962 after meeting at church social in 1961 (Q01, Q02), Harold worked at Ford plant with union membership (Q01, Q66), three children: Robert (62, Ann Arbor), James (59, Ohio), Patricia (56, Ypsilanti), seven grandchildren, three great-grandchildren (Q05).

Seed mentions Saline, Washtenaw County as current location. Interview adds: moved to Saline in 1978 from Tecumseh area (Q02), bought house that year (Q58), mortgage paid off in 2003. Household income approximately $3,000/month from Ford pension and government retirement benefits (Q74). Three-bedroom home (Q11).

Seed mentions 'partial college studies in business administration.' Interview specifies: attended community college for about two years (Q90), left to marry Harold in 1962 at age 20 (Q02), aspires to finish certificate online (Q01 follow-up, Q91). Father's educational attainment (high school, Q100) and mother's (high school, Q101) contextualize her as first in family to attempt higher education.

behavioral_economist.detail: Seed states 'meticulous budgeting, record-keeping, and methodical planning.' Interview provides deep behavioral evidence: handwritten ledger maintained 50+ years, envelope system for different expense categories (house fund, holiday fund, medical fund), monthly reconciliation against budget (Q56, Q59, Q61). Traces origin to watching mother discuss store economics (Q103 follow-up).

Seed describes 'competitive edge drives her to double-check every detail.' Interview confirms and contextualizes: keeps running score notebook at bridge (Q07 follow-up), double-checks crossword answers (Q01), checks every receipt and ledger entry (Q56, Q65), verifies pill compliance with refrigerator chart (Q31 follow-up). Competition is self-directed and accuracy-focused, not interpersonally aggressive.

Seed mentions 'senior discounts to keep expenses predictable.' Interview quantifies financial picture: ~$3,000/month fixed income (Q74), ~$2,000/month typical spending excluding quarterly property tax (Q57), $60-70/month medication co-pays (Q36, Q40), $50/month house repair fund (Q58 follow-up), small savings contributions of $40-50/month (Q61 follow-up). Zero debt (Q63). Can absorb $400 emergency (Q82).

Seed describes 'thrift-driven palate' and 'simple, seasonal dishes.' Interview confirms loss-aversion-driven food behavior: cooks from garden to reduce grocery costs (Q64, Q81), delays fabric purchases when budget is tight (Q60), chose cheaper medication over more effective but expensive alternative (Q36). Every spending decision framed as opportunity cost analysis.

Seed mentions 'community finance volunteer.' Interview reveals scope: 8 years at senior center reconciling donations, tracking expenses, preparing event budgets, maintaining paper ledger alongside Linda's computer records (Q16 follow-up). Also managed Harold's carpentry side-business books (Q65 follow-up) and church women's group treasury for 20+ years (Q01, Q47).

political_scientist.detail: Seed contains no political information. Interview provides rich civic profile: votes in every election since age 21 (Q23), researches candidates via local paper, voter guides, TV debates (Q23 follow-up), writes letters to representatives on prescription costs and road funding (Q27 follow-up). Avoids partisan identification, describes self as issue-oriented (Q95, Q96).

Seed cultural_background mentions 'church, local civic groups.' Interview elaborates deep civic embedding: 48-year Saline resident, church fellowship organizer, library volunteer for 12 years, senior center finance volunteer for 8 years, walking club member (Q07, Q16, Q47). Civic participation is institutional and habitual rather than activist-oriented.

Seed has no media/information data. Interview reveals information diet: Saline Post (delivered), Ann Arbor News (library), broadcast evening news at 6 PM, local morning radio; stopped watching cable news due to partisan tone (Q24 follow-up). Bridge group imposed 'no politics' rule (Q24 follow-up). Trusts doctor and local pharmacist over internet for health information (Q42).

Seed has no racial/social justice content. Interview reveals limited but reflective engagement: acknowledges homogeneous upbringing (Q26, Q28), listens to grandchildren's perspectives on BLM and racial justice (Q26 follow-up, Q30), signed church statement on human dignity (Q27 follow-up). Default framework is 'treat everyone with respect' rather than structural analysis (Q27, Q29).

psychologist.detail: Seed describes 'quiet competitive streak' and 'double-checking crossword answers and recipe measurements.' Interview reveals deep behavioral pattern: keeps 20-year running bridge scorebook (Q07 follow-up), tracks bird sightings in notebook (Q17), maintains handwritten ledger and pill compliance chart (Q31, Q56). The double-checking behavior is pervasive across domains -- quilting stitches, tax returns, crosswords -- and tied to identity as someone who 'does things right' (Q106 follow-up, Q109).

Seed persona states 'steadfast dedication to order' and 'rigid daily routine.' Interview reveals this is a self-regulation mechanism: routine is 'my anchor' (Q43 follow-up), sticking to schedule combats low mood (Q45, Q50), loss of routine during illness triggered crying episode and existential fear (Q44). The rigidity is functional, not merely preferential.

Seed skills mention 'questioning assumptions to ensure quality.' Interview contextualizes: challenges doctors on pill necessity (Q39 follow-up), told grandson to get vaccinated despite his hesitancy (Q41 follow-up), questioned moving to Ohio despite son's suggestion (Q10 follow-up). Pattern is assertive within trusted relationships but avoidant in public/political contexts (Q29).

Seed mentions no emotional distress patterns. Interview reveals: nighttime worry spirals about health, finances, Harold's mortality (Q45 follow-up), seasonal mood decline in winter (Q32, Q43, Q45), crying episode during illness reflecting fear of dependency and uselessness (Q44), generational survivor grief (Q43). Coping is exclusively behavioral: cleaning, organizing, quilting, list-making (Q38, Q45). Actively resists discussing feelings with family (Q44 follow-up, Q45 follow-up).

Seed mentions preference for 'predictable, low-intensity exercise.' Interview reveals the predictability need extends to all domains: same breakfast daily (Q15), supper at 5:00 sharp (Q15), bed by 9 PM (Q15 follow-up), appointments at same time each week (Q78), grocery shopping by list aisle-by-aisle (Q18). Deviation from predictability causes measurable distress (Q18 follow-up).

Seed arts_persona mentions 'crocheted patterns' as a creative outlet. Interview focuses exclusively on quilting and knitting (Q11, Q17); crochet is never mentioned by the persona. This may indicate crochet is inactive or conflated with knitting in the seed. Flagged as unresolved in extracted profile.

direction_2_interview_vs_extracted_profi.description: Interview transcript compared against the extracted_profile for completeness, accuracy, and misattribution.

demographer.detail: Profile header correctly states: participant_id, name, age 85, gender Female, married_present, Saline MI 48176, some college (business administration, not completed), not in workforce (retired homemaker, active volunteer). All match interview data (Q84-Q90, Q01, Q16).

Demographer summary correctly identifies: white woman of German descent, born/raised Tecumseh MI, now Saline; 64-year marriage, three children, seven grandchildren, three great-grandchildren; some college not completed; fixed income ~$3,000/month; home owned outright. All supported by Q01, Q05, Q58, Q74, Q89.

Profile others_misc life_events lists 'father ~1992' for death. Interview says 'my father a few years before that' relative to mother's 1995 death (Q06). The ~1992 is an approximation that could be off; Deeva never gives an exact date. Should note as estimated.

Profile correctly captures birthplace as Tecumseh, Michigan (Q87), childhood at age 16 in Tecumseh (Q97), parents' education levels (Q100, Q101), mother's occupation at Woolworth's (Q102-Q104), father's occupation at grain elevator (Q105-Q107).

behavioral_economist.detail: Profile home_and_lifestyle correctly captures envelope system, handwritten ledger, $50/month house fund. Financial data in work_and_career and home_and_lifestyle accurately reflects interview (Q56, Q58, Q61). No misattributions found.

Profile does not include a dedicated 'finances' preference category. Financial behaviors are scattered across home_and_lifestyle, work_and_career, and the behavioral_economist summary. Interview provides rich standalone financial data: $3,000/month income (Q74), ~$2,000/month expenses (Q57), zero debt (Q63), $400 emergency capacity (Q82), medication cost management (Q36), grocery cost-cutting strategies (Q60, Q81). This warrants consolidation or at minimum cross-referencing.

Profile behavioral_economist summary correctly identifies: loss aversion, high need for financial control, needs-over-wants prioritization, low risk tolerance (declined expensive medication), identity tied to financial competence. All supported by Q36, Q56, Q61, Q64, Q83.

Profile does not capture the specific coping-through-austerity sequence Deeva describes: when unexpected expenses hit, she follows a strict hierarchy of cuts (fabric > books/meals out > grocery reduction > never medications/utilities) (Q64 follow-up). This reveals a clear preference ordering that the behavioral_economist summary should reference.

political_scientist.detail: Profile community_and_civic correctly captures: votes in every election since age 21, researches candidates via local paper/voter guides/TV debates, writes letters to representatives on prescription costs and road funding, organizes church fellowship, volunteers weekly. All match Q23, Q24, Q27, Q16, Q47.

Profile political_scientist summary correctly describes her as 'issue-oriented rather than party-loyal' and identifies fiscal conservatism as primary value. However, it does not capture her specific policy priorities as stated in Q24 follow-up: retirement benefits, senior program funding, prescription drug costs, property taxes, local school board quality, road infrastructure. These are her voting decision criteria.

Profile others_misc media_and_information_diet accurately lists: Saline Post, Ann Arbor News, broadcast evening news, local morning radio, stopped cable news, passive Facebook, trusts doctor/pharmacist over internet. Matches Q24 follow-up, Q42, Q48.

Profile others_misc unresolved correctly flags the party lean ambiguity: 'behavioral signals suggest moderate-to-center-right but not confirmed.' This accurately reflects Q95 and Q96 where Deeva avoids labeling herself.

Profile political_scientist summary does not mention Deeva's growing disillusionment with partisan polarization (Q24 follow-up, Q25) or her specific concern that 'nothing gets done because everyone's fighting' (Q25). This is a significant attitudinal shift that contextualizes her civic engagement pattern.

psychologist.detail: Profile psychologist summary correctly identifies Big Five behavioral patterns: high Conscientiousness, moderate-to-low Openness, moderate Agreeableness with competitive streak, moderate-to-low Extraversion, moderate Neuroticism. All behavioral evidence is properly cited. The summary is behavioral-only with no clinical labels.

Profile correctly identifies behavioral activation as primary coping mechanism (cleaning, organizing, quilting during Harold's hospital stay per Q38 follow-up), self-reliance/resistance to dependency (Q18 follow-up, Q44 follow-up), and quiet endurance over verbal expression (Q43 follow-up, Q45 follow-up).

Profile psychologist summary mentions 'nighttime rumination' but does not capture the specific behavioral pattern Deeva describes: lying in bed running through worst-case health/financial scenarios, then getting up to write lists as a coping mechanism (Q45 follow-up). The list-making-as-anxiety-management is a distinctive and therapeutically relevant behavioral pattern.

Profile does not capture the generational survivor dimension of Deeva's emotional landscape: she is aware of being 'one of the last ones standing' from her generation (Q43), both brothers gone, many friends gone. This chronic low-level grief shapes her daily emotional baseline and is separate from the acute worry about Harold's health.

Profile correctly identifies self-concept built around competence, reliability, and usefulness, with loss of these capacities as deepest vulnerability. This is powerfully confirmed by Q44 (crying episode: 'Is this what the rest of my life is going to be? Sitting in a chair') and Q18 follow-up (frustration at dependency).

direction_3_profile_internal_consistency.description: Extracted profile checked for internal contradictions across sections.

demographer.detail: Profile header states 'married_present.' Social_and_relationships details confirm 64-year marriage to Harold. Home_and_lifestyle confirms two-person household. Work_and_career references Harold's carpentry books. All internally consistent.

Profile header states age 85. Health_and_wellness references age-appropriate conditions (knee issues, vision decline). Travel details mention needing family to drive. Work_and_career notes volunteer-only status. All age-consistent.

Profile sports_and_fitness states 'Occasionally participates in senior softball league' (from seed data). Profile health_and_wellness and others_misc sensory_and_physical describe bilateral knee stiffness limiting mobility, requiring medication change 18 months ago, and inability to garden for extended periods. Active softball participation at 85 with this knee condition is internally inconsistent. The unresolved section flags this but the sports_and_fitness section still lists it as active.

behavioral_economist.detail: Profile financial behaviors are internally coherent: envelope system (home_and_lifestyle) aligns with zero debt (not explicitly in profile but in summary), $50/month house fund (home_and_lifestyle), medication budgeting (health_and_wellness). No contradictions in spending or saving patterns across sections.

Profile food_and_dining (thrift-driven, garden-based meals) aligns with nature_and_outdoors (garden as food source) and health_and_wellness (low-salt diet using garden herbs). The cross-section references are mutually reinforcing.

Profile work_and_career (volunteer rigor, punctuality) aligns with psychologist summary (high Conscientiousness, meticulous record-keeping). The behavioral economist summary's identification of identity tied to financial competence is consistent with work_and_career's emphasis on bookkeeping expertise.

political_scientist.detail: Profile community_and_civic (votes every election, writes to representatives) aligns with political_scientist summary (highly engaged civic participant, conventional participation). Media diet in others_misc (avoids cable news, reads local paper) is consistent with the 'information-moderate' characterization.

Profile others_misc unresolved correctly flags party lean as ambiguous, while political_scientist summary describes her as 'issue-oriented rather than party-loyal.' These are mutually consistent framings of the same deliberate ambiguity.

psychologist.detail: Profile psychologist summary's 'moderate-to-low Extraversion' is consistent with social_and_relationships (small-group preference, four-person bridge group, handwritten letters over digital) and technology (minimal social media, distrusts internet, prefers in-person). No contradictions.

Profile psychologist summary's 'moderate Neuroticism' (nighttime rumination, health worry, effective coping) is consistent with health_and_wellness (strict pill routine, careful about illness prevention) and the behavioral economist summary's identification of loss aversion. The worry drives preventive behavior, which is coherent.

Profile psychologist summary states 'moderate Agreeableness' with competitive streak. The social_and_relationships section emphasizes warmth and community care. These are not contradictory but the nuance could be sharper: Deeva is highly agreeable in community service contexts but competitive in task/accuracy contexts (bridge scores, budget precision, quilting quality). The competitive streak is domain-specific, not a general interpersonal pattern.

direction_4_completeness.description: Checking whether the extracted profile captures all significant information from the interview.

demographer.detail: Profile does not capture Deeva's birthplace of Tecumseh, Michigan (Q87) or that she lived there until the 1978 move to Saline (Q97). This is important demographic context -- she grew up in a different Michigan town, not Saline.

Profile does not capture parents' specific occupations: father at grain elevator/feed mill (Q106), mother at Woolworth's five-and-dime (Q103). These occupations contextualize the working-class Midwestern background and the origin of Deeva's financial literacy.

Profile does not capture sibling details: two older brothers Walter (died 2010) and Frank (died 2016, in care facility for 2 years before death) (Q06, Q35). Harold's sister in Indiana is also not mentioned (Q06). These family losses shape her current emotional state.

Profile demographer summary does not mention Harold's age (87, per Q05) or his hearing decline and limited mobility (Q18 follow-up). As a two-person household where both members are elderly, Harold's health status is demographically significant.

Profile does not capture that Deeva was born in 1941 (Q85) or that Harold worked specifically at a Ford plant (Q01, Q66), which provided union benefits and pension that currently sustain the household. The Ford pension is a critical income source.

behavioral_economist.detail: Profile does not capture the specific expense hierarchy Deeva would cut in financial distress: fabric and books first, then meals out, then grocery reduction via soups/garden meals, with medications and utilities as untouchable (Q64 follow-up). This is a revealed preference ordering.

Profile does not capture the children's financial support pattern: Robert and Patricia split Harold's uncovered procedure costs (Q40, Q60 follow-up) without being asked. Deeva finds this 'grateful but uncomfortable' (Q40 follow-up). This intergenerational transfer pattern is a significant financial behavior.

Profile does not capture Deeva's tax filing behavior: files paper returns herself, Patricia double-checks math, enjoys the process as a 'puzzle with a right answer,' has never made an error (Q75). This reinforces the identity-as-financial-competence pattern.

Profile mentions small savings account and emergency fund in home_and_lifestyle details, but does not quantify: savings at credit union for decades, cash reserve at home, contributions of $40-50/month down from $100+ when Harold worked, account depleted by procedure and roof repair but being rebuilt (Q61, Q61 follow-up). The quantification matters for understanding their financial precarity.

political_scientist.detail: Profile does not list Deeva's specific policy priorities: retirement/Social Security benefits, senior program funding, prescription drug costs, property taxes, local school board quality, Michigan road infrastructure (Q24 follow-up, Q25, Q83 follow-up). These are her concrete voting decision criteria.

Profile does not capture Deeva's growing political disillusionment: 'more discouraged than I used to be,' 'nothing gets done because everyone's fighting,' frustrated by rising costs with no government action (Q25). This is a significant attitudinal shift in recent years.

Profile does not capture the intergenerational political learning: granddaughter opened her eyes to student loan costs and environmental concerns, Deeva now brings reusable bags to store (Q25 follow-up). This shows late-life openness to new civic perspectives despite generally traditional orientation.

psychologist.detail: Profile does not capture the specific list-making-as-anxiety-management behavior: Deeva gets out of bed during nighttime worry spirals, writes down fears and actionable steps, then can sleep (Q45 follow-up). This is a distinctive cognitive-behavioral strategy she developed independently.

Profile does not capture the generational survivor grief dimension: awareness of being 'one of the last ones standing,' both brothers gone, many friends gone, 'loneliness of being one of the last ones standing, it's real' (Q43). This is distinct from the worry about Harold and shapes her daily baseline mood.

Profile does not capture Deeva's relationship with dependency and burden: 'I don't like being a burden, even though Patricia says I'm not one' (Q18 follow-up), hid tears from Patricia during illness (Q44 follow-up), reluctance to accept financial help from children (Q40 follow-up, Q60 follow-up). This burden-avoidance is a core behavioral driver, not just a preference.

Profile does not capture the faith-as-coping dimension in behavioral terms: evening prayers asking for family health and guidance (Q47), devotional booklet nightly (Q47 follow-up), spiritual grounding in garden and seasonal cycles (Q47 follow-up), church community as practical support network during crises (Q47 follow-up). These are active behavioral practices, not merely stated beliefs.

Profile does not capture the emotional significance of routine loss: during the 3-week illness, missing bridge, volunteering, and gardening caused more distress than the physical illness itself (Q44). Deeva's self-worth is so tied to activity and usefulness that inactivity itself becomes the primary psychological threat.

expert_summaries.demographer: Deeva Cintron is an 85-year-old white woman of German descent, born in Tecumseh, Michigan in 1941 (Q85, Q87, Q89), now living in Saline, Washtenaw County since 1978 (Q02, Q08). She has been married to Harold (87) for 64 years (Q84); they have three adult children -- Robert (62, Ann Arbor), James (59, Ohio), Patricia (56, Ypsilanti) -- seven grandchildren, and three great-grandchildren (Q05). She completed approximately two years of community college in business administration before leaving to marry in 1962 (Q01, Q02, Q90). The household income is approximately $3,000/month from Harold's Ford union pension and government retirement benefits (Q66, Q74), with a mortgage-free three-bedroom home purchased in 1978 and paid off in 2003 (Q58). Her demographic profile is characteristic of aging Midwestern white working-class women: second/third-generation European immigrant heritage, strong church and civic ties, modest education interrupted by marriage, long-duration stable marriage, and deep rootedness in a small community.
expert_summaries.behavioral_economist: Deeva exhibits pronounced loss aversion and an exceptionally high need for financial control, manifested through a handwritten ledger maintained for 50+ years, an envelope system for categorical budgeting (house fund, holiday fund, medical fund), and monthly reconciliation against planned budgets (Q56, Q59, Q61). Her spending hierarchy in financial distress reveals clear preference ordering: discretionary items (fabric, books) are cut first, followed by grocery reduction via garden and pantry meals, while medications and utilities are treated as inviolable (Q64 follow-up). She declined a more effective but expensive knee medication in favor of a cheaper alternative, demonstrating risk-averse cost optimization over health maximization (Q36). Her financial identity is self-sustaining -- she derives visible satisfaction from being 'the numbers person' (Q11, Q65, Q83), files her own paper tax returns as an enjoyable activity (Q75), and experiences accepting financial help from children as a threat to autonomy (Q40 follow-up). The Ford pension and government benefits provide approximately $3,000/month against ~$2,000/month in regular expenses (Q57, Q74), with zero debt (Q63) and a modest savings buffer that can absorb a $400 emergency without borrowing (Q82).
expert_summaries.political_scientist: Deeva is a highly engaged conventional civic participant who has voted in every election since age 21 -- federal, state, local, and school board -- reflecting a deep sense of civic duty transmitted through family socialization: 'If you don't vote, you don't get to complain' (Q23, Q23 follow-up). She researches candidates via the Saline Post, Ann Arbor News, voter guides, and broadcast evening news, having abandoned cable news due to partisan tone (Q24 follow-up). Her policy priorities center on bread-and-butter issues affecting her daily life: prescription drug costs, retirement benefit protection, property taxes, school board quality, and Michigan road infrastructure (Q24 follow-up, Q25, Q83 follow-up). She describes herself as issue-oriented rather than party-loyal, with fiscal conservatism as her primary value tempered by support for community programs benefiting seniors (Q95, Q96). She writes occasional letters to representatives but engages in no protest activity (Q27 follow-up). On racial justice issues, she defaults to an interpersonal 'treat everyone with respect' framework rather than structural analysis, acknowledging the limits of her homogeneous small-town perspective (Q26, Q27, Q28). She expresses growing disillusionment with partisan polarization -- 'nothing gets done because everyone's fighting' -- and has experienced late-life perspective shifts through conversations with grandchildren about student debt and environmentalism (Q25, Q25 follow-up).
expert_summaries.psychologist: Deeva's behavioral profile centers on exceptionally high Conscientiousness expressed across every life domain: meticulous record-keeping (50-year ledger, bridge scorebook, bird-watching notebook, pill compliance chart), rigid temporal structuring (same wake time, meal times, bed time daily; fixed weekly schedule with each day assigned specific tasks), and pervasive double-checking (crossword answers, quilting stitches, tax returns, budget reconciliation) (Q03, Q07 follow-up, Q15, Q17, Q56). Her routine functions as a self-regulation mechanism rather than mere preference -- during a 3-week illness that disrupted all activities, she experienced an acute identity crisis ('Is this what the rest of my life is going to be?'), demonstrating that activity and usefulness are constitutive of her self-concept, not accessories to it (Q44). She manages nighttime worry spirals (health catastrophizing, financial scenarios, Harold's mortality) through a self-developed behavioral strategy: getting out of bed and writing itemized lists of fears and actionable steps (Q45 follow-up). She exhibits strong self-reliance and burden-avoidance, hiding tears from Patricia during illness and experiencing discomfort when accepting financial help from children (Q44 follow-up, Q40 follow-up). She processes the chronic grief of generational survivorship -- both brothers, both parents, and many friends gone -- through quiet endurance, faith practices (evening prayer, devotional reading, church attendance), and commitment to remaining useful to her community (Q43, Q47). Her competitive streak is domain-specific (bridge scores, budget precision, quilting quality) rather than interpersonally aggressive, serving as competence demonstration rather than social dominance (Q07 follow-up, Q65).

inconsistencies_found.description: Senior softball league listed as active in sports_and_fitness despite severe bilateral knee condition requiring medication change 18 months ago. At 85 with documented mobility limitations (cannot garden for extended periods, limits stair use, cannot stand long in grocery store), active softball is implausible.
inconsistencies_found.source_refs: Seed sports_persona; Q31, Q33, Q34, Q18
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Remove from active activities in sports_and_fitness. Note as historically participated but likely inactive given current physical condition. This aligns with the unresolved flag already present in the profile.

inconsistencies_found.description: Profile arts_and_culture mentions 'integrates artistic influences into quilting and crochet patterns.' Interview only discusses quilting and knitting; crochet is never mentioned by Deeva (Q11, Q17). Seed arts_persona mentions 'crocheted patterns' but this may be conflated with knitting.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Change 'crochet' to 'knitting' in arts_and_culture to match interview evidence. Note crochet as unresolved/possibly inactive in others_misc.

inconsistencies_found.description: Profile travel evidence cites 'Q108 (seed data: travel_persona)' -- the travel preferences come almost entirely from seed data rather than interview evidence. Q108 confirms the Quilt Museum dream and Mackinac Island wish, but other destinations (Pictured Rocks, Charleston SC) are seed-only.
inconsistencies_found.resolution: Retain all travel details but update evidence to distinguish seed-sourced items from interview-confirmed items. Add Q10 follow-up (rejected moving from Saline) as evidence of strong place attachment relevant to travel behavior.

resolutions.action: Removed 'Occasionally participates in senior softball league' from sports_and_fitness active details. Added note that softball was referenced in seed but is likely inactive given current knee condition and mobility limitations documented in Q31, Q33, Q34.
Changed 'crochet patterns' to 'knitting patterns' in arts_and_culture. Retained crochet in others_misc unresolved section with note that it may be conflated with knitting or no longer practiced.

Updated travel evidence citations to distinguish seed-sourced (Pictured Rocks, Charleston SC) from interview-confirmed (Mackinac Island return, National Quilt Museum dream). Added Q10 follow-up as evidence for place attachment.

gaps_addressed.action: Added birthplace Tecumseh, Michigan and pre-1978 residence history to demographics header and demographer summary.
Added parents' occupations (father: grain elevator/feed mill; mother: Woolworth's five-and-dime) to others_misc life_events_and_transitions.
Added sibling details (Walter d.2010, Frank d.2016 after 2 years in care facility) and Harold's sister in Indiana to social_and_relationships and others_misc life_events_and_transitions.
Added Harold's age (87), hearing decline, and reduced mobility to social_and_relationships details.
Added DOB 1941, Harold's Ford plant employment, and Ford pension as income source to demographics and work_and_career.
Added expense-cutting hierarchy (discretionary first, then groceries, medications/utilities untouchable) to home_and_lifestyle details.
Added children's financial support pattern (Robert and Patricia splitting uncovered medical costs) to social_and_relationships details.
Added paper tax filing behavior and enjoyment of the process to work_and_career details.
Added savings quantification (credit union account, cash at home, $40-50/month contributions, depleted by procedure/roof but rebuilding) to home_and_lifestyle details.
Added specific policy priorities (prescription costs, retirement benefits, property taxes, school board, road infrastructure) to community_and_civic details.
Added growing political disillusionment to community_and_civic details and political_scientist summary.
Added intergenerational civic learning (granddaughter's influence on environmental awareness) to community_and_civic details.
Added list-making-as-anxiety-management behavior to psychologist summary and health_and_wellness details.
Added generational survivor grief to psychologist summary and others_misc life_events_and_transitions.
Added burden-avoidance pattern (hiding tears, reluctance to accept help) to psychologist summary and social_and_relationships.
Added faith-as-coping behavioral practices to others_misc cultural_and_linguistic and psychologist summary.
Added emotional significance of routine loss to psychologist summary.

verification.overall_verdict: The Step 2 extracted profile for Deeva Cintron is fundamentally sound. The demographics header, 15 preference categories, 4 expert summaries, and 6 others_misc subcategories are all present and structurally complete. The interview transcript is richly detailed across all 109 questions and the profile captures the core persona accurately. However, three categories of issues require correction: (1) One factual inconsistency -- senior softball listed as active despite documented severe knee condition at age 85; (2) One seed-vs-interview discrepancy -- crochet referenced in seed but only quilting and knitting discussed in interview; (3) Approximately 17 completeness gaps where significant interview data was not captured in the profile, concentrated in financial detail quantification, family background specifics, policy priorities, and psychological coping mechanisms. The corrected profile addresses all three categories while preserving everything that was correct in the original.

corrected_extracted_profile.name: Deeva Cintron
corrected_extracted_profile.education: Some college (business administration at community college, ~2 years, not completed; left in 1962 to marry)
corrected_extracted_profile.occupation: Not in workforce (retired homemaker; active volunteer at senior center, library, and church)

health_and_wellness.summary: Maintains strict daily pill routine and low-salt diet; walks twice weekly; avoids unnecessary medications; manages nighttime worry through list-making
details: Takes joint medication and cardiovascular-related pills morning and evening using a weekly pill organizer filled every Sunday night
Follows low-salt diet prescribed by doctor, substituting garden herbs (rosemary, thyme, garlic, lemon juice) for salt
Walks 1.5-2 miles twice weekly (Tuesdays and Thursdays, 8 AM) with senior walking club on flat trail behind library; 30-40 minutes at steady pace
Does gentle knee stretches at kitchen counter on cold mornings; walks inside house when ice prevents outdoor walking
Gets flu shot and all recommended vaccinations annually; got every pandemic vaccine round
Takes doctor-recommended immune supplement since chest illness recovery
Uses home remedies passed down from mother: chamomile tea for sleep, peppermint oil for headaches, honey-lemon for colds, salt water gargle for sore throat
Sees Dr. Hendricks in Ann Arbor every few months for blood-level checks (15-year patient relationship)
Manages nighttime health/financial worry spirals by getting up and writing itemized lists of fears and actionable steps
Takes warm shower on cold mornings to loosen joints before activity

summaries.demographer: Deeva Cintron is an 85-year-old white woman of German descent, born in Tecumseh, Michigan in 1941 (Q85, Q87, Q89), now living in Saline, Washtenaw County since 1978 (Q02, Q08). She has been married to Harold (87) for 64 years (Q84); they have three adult children -- Robert (62, Ann Arbor), James (59, Ohio), Patricia (56, Ypsilanti) -- seven grandchildren, and three great-grandchildren (Q05). Both brothers are deceased: Walter (d.2010) and Frank (d.2016 after two years in a care facility) (Q06, Q35). She completed approximately two years of community college in business administration before leaving to marry in 1962 (Q01, Q02, Q90), following both parents who had high school educations -- her father worked at a grain elevator/feed mill and her mother at Woolworth's five-and-dime (Q100-Q107). The household income is approximately $3,000/month from Harold's Ford union pension and government retirement benefits (Q66, Q74), with a mortgage-free three-bedroom home purchased in 1978 and paid off in 2003 (Q58). Her demographic profile is characteristic of aging Midwestern white working-class women: second/third-generation European immigrant heritage, strong church and civic ties, modest education interrupted by marriage, long-duration stable marriage, and deep rootedness in a small community.
summaries.behavioral_economist: Deeva exhibits pronounced loss aversion and an exceptionally high need for financial control, manifested through a handwritten ledger maintained for 50+ years, an envelope system for categorical budgeting (house fund, holiday fund, medical fund), and monthly reconciliation against planned budgets (Q56, Q59, Q61). Her spending hierarchy in financial distress reveals clear preference ordering: discretionary items (fabric, books) are cut first, followed by grocery reduction via garden and pantry meals, while medications and utilities are treated as inviolable (Q64 follow-up). She declined a more effective but expensive knee medication in favor of a cheaper alternative, demonstrating risk-averse cost optimization over health maximization (Q36). Her financial identity is self-sustaining -- she derives visible satisfaction from being 'the numbers person' (Q11, Q65, Q83), files her own paper tax returns as an enjoyable activity (Q75), and experiences accepting financial help from children as a threat to autonomy (Q40 follow-up). The Ford pension and government benefits provide approximately $3,000/month against ~$2,000/month in regular expenses (Q57, Q74), with zero debt (Q63) and a modest savings buffer that can absorb a $400 emergency without borrowing (Q82). The primary financial risk is a major health event: Harold's prior procedure required children to cover uncovered costs of ~$2,000 (Q60 follow-up), and Deeva's biggest financial fear is 'running out of money before we run out of time' (Q83).
summaries.political_scientist: Deeva is a highly engaged conventional civic participant who has voted in every election since age 21 -- federal, state, local, and school board -- reflecting a deep sense of civic duty transmitted through family socialization: 'If you don't vote, you don't get to complain' (Q23, Q23 follow-up). She researches candidates via the Saline Post, Ann Arbor News, voter guides, and broadcast evening news, having abandoned cable news due to partisan tone (Q24 follow-up). Her policy priorities center on bread-and-butter issues affecting her daily life: prescription drug costs, retirement benefit protection, property taxes, school board quality, and Michigan road infrastructure (Q24 follow-up, Q25, Q83 follow-up). She describes herself as issue-oriented rather than party-loyal, with fiscal conservatism as her primary value tempered by support for community programs benefiting seniors (Q95, Q96). She writes occasional letters to representatives but engages in no protest activity (Q27 follow-up). On racial justice issues, she defaults to an interpersonal 'treat everyone with respect' framework rather than structural analysis, acknowledging the limits of her homogeneous small-town perspective (Q26, Q27, Q28). She expresses growing disillusionment with partisan polarization -- 'nothing gets done because everyone's fighting' -- and has experienced late-life perspective shifts through conversations with grandchildren about student debt and environmentalism (Q25, Q25 follow-up).
summaries.psychologist: Deeva's behavioral profile centers on exceptionally high Conscientiousness expressed across every life domain: meticulous record-keeping (50-year ledger, bridge scorebook, bird-watching notebook, pill compliance chart), rigid temporal structuring (same wake time, meal times, bed time daily; fixed weekly schedule with each day assigned specific tasks), and pervasive double-checking (crossword answers, quilting stitches, tax returns, budget reconciliation) (Q03, Q07 follow-up, Q15, Q17, Q56). Her routine functions as a self-regulation mechanism rather than mere preference -- during a 3-week illness that disrupted all activities, she experienced an acute identity crisis ('Is this what the rest of my life is going to be?'), demonstrating that activity and usefulness are constitutive of her self-concept, not accessories to it (Q44). She manages nighttime worry spirals (health catastrophizing, financial scenarios, Harold's mortality) through a self-developed behavioral strategy: getting out of bed and writing itemized lists of fears and actionable steps (Q45 follow-up). She exhibits strong self-reliance and burden-avoidance, hiding tears from Patricia during illness and experiencing discomfort when accepting financial help from children (Q44 follow-up, Q40 follow-up). She processes the chronic grief of generational survivorship -- both brothers, both parents, and many friends gone -- through quiet endurance, faith practices (evening prayer, devotional reading, church attendance), and commitment to remaining useful to her community (Q43, Q47). Her competitive streak is domain-specific (bridge scores, budget precision, quilting quality) rather than interpersonally aggressive, serving as competence demonstration rather than social dominance (Q07 follow-up, Q65).
